beats.text: A game is a model of interaction between agents whose outcomes depend on each other's choices, not a pastime.
The minimal data is a triple: a set of players, a strategy set for each, and a payoff function over every profile.
The feature that breaks ordinary optimization is that each player's payoff depends on the whole profile, not just their own move.
In the Prisoner's Dilemma each suspect cooperates or defects, and mutual cooperation pays each player two.
The best response holds everyone else fixed and selects the strategies that maximize a player's own payoff.
Against a cooperating opponent, defecting pays three rather than two, which is exactly why cooperation is unstable.
Sometimes one strategy is better no matter what anyone else does, which makes the opponent's reasoning irrelevant.

visual.sub: When one move beats all others

beats.text: A strategy strictly dominates another when it pays strictly more against every possible choice of the opponents.

visual.tex: u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) > u_i(s_i', s_{-i}) \quad \text{for every } s_{-i}

beats.text: In the Prisoner's Dilemma defect beats cooperate against a cooperator by three to two and against a defector by one to zero.
Both players have a dominant strategy of defect, so the predicted outcome is mutual defection paying one each.
Individual rationality drives the pair to one each even though both would have preferred the unstable two each.
Iterated elimination deletes dominated strategies in turn, and if one survives per player the game is dominance solvable.
Nash's 1950 contribution handles every game through mutual stability, not just the ones dominance can solve.
A profile is a Nash equilibrium when no player can improve their payoff by changing only their own strategy.

visual.tex: u_i(s_i^\star, s_{-i}^\star) \ge u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^\star) \quad \text{for every } i, s_i

beats.text: In the Prisoner's Dilemma mutual defect passes the test, since unilaterally cooperating drops a player from one to zero.
Equilibrium is not optimality, since mutual defection is Pareto dominated by the cooperative outcome both players prefer.
Equilibria need not be unique, and the theory alone does not say which one rational players will land on.
Nash's theorem guarantees every finite game has at least one equilibrium once mixed strategies are allowed.

visual.note: Fixed point of mutual best response

beats.text: A two-player zero-sum game is pure conflict, where whatever one player wins the other loses in every cell.
The row player guarantees the maximin, the column player concedes at most the minimax, and the first never exceeds the second.
For Matching Pennies the pure maximin is minus one and the pure minimax is plus one, leaving a gap of two.
Von Neumann's theorem says once mixed strategies are allowed the maximin and minimax are always equal.

visual.tex: \max_{p} \min_{q} \, p^{\top} A\, q = \min_{q} \max_{p} \, p^{\top} A\, q = v

beats.text: Their common value is the value of the game, and the optimal mixed strategies are interchangeable and worst-case optimal.
Zero-sum is uniquely tractable, with a unique value and an optimal strategy computable by linear programming.
Matching Pennies has no pure equilibrium, since any pure choice can be exploited, so the escape is to randomize.
A mixed strategy is a probability distribution over actions, and for two actions it is the segment of mixes p and one minus p.
The indifference principle pins your mix by making the opponent indifferent across all the actions they use.

visual.tex: p^\star = \frac{d - c}{a - b - c + d}, \quad q^\star = \frac{d - b}{a - b - c + d}

beats.text: For Matching Pennies the denominator is four, so each player plays heads and tails with probability one half.
With the skewed matrix four, zero, one, three the top row probability is two over six, about one third.
At the equilibrium mix neither player can be exploited, which is the precise sense a random strategy can be safer.
